reasoning: The query at line 335 interpolates only $wpdb->posts, which is a WordPress-managed table-name constant derived from the configured DB prefix at install time — never from user input. The full SQL string is otherwise a static literal (fixed WHERE clauses with string literals 'attachment' and '%%image%%'). No attacker-controlled data reaches this query at any point in its call chain. The call site at line 468 is the else-branch of admin_enqueues(), which is hooked to admin_enqueue_scripts (fires only for logged-in admin users) and additionally gated by a hook_suffix check (line 106) that returns early unless the current admin page matches $this->menu_id. There is no path by which an unauthenticated or low-privilege user can trigger this code. The absence of $wpdb->prepare() is a WordPress coding-standard violation, but since no variable data is ever interpolated into the query, it carries zero exploitable SQLi risk in its current form. The finding is a false positive in terms of real vulnerability; it is at most a static-analysis / code-quality note.
